try_capstone_scope_and_architecture_sket.title: Try it yourself
try_capstone_scope_and_architecture_sket.prompt: Create `README.md` and a tiny architecture check script output through `main.py`.

try_capstone_scope_and_architecture_sket.title: Try it yourself
try_capstone_scope_and_architecture_sket.prompt: Create `models/base_task.py` with a base class that stores common task state.

ci-capstone-architecture-practice.title: Add service and model folders for the capstone
ci-capstone-architecture-practice.prompt: Create a tiny `services/reports.py` function that proves the project can import service helpers.
ci-capstone-architecture-practice.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

sc-capstone-scope-and-architecture-desig.title: Check the design choice
sc-capstone-scope-and-architecture-desig.prompt: Which choice best matches the lesson goal for capstone scope and architecture?
sc-capstone-scope-and-architecture-desig.hint: Choose the option that keeps object responsibilities clear.

help.concept: Plan a real multi-file OOP app before writing the final classes and services.
help.hint_1: Look for the answer that reduces duplication without hiding behavior.
help.hint_2: A good OOP answer usually keeps behavior near the object that owns the data.

options.a: Put every class in main.py so imports are never needed.
options.b: Give each class or service a focused responsibility and import it where needed.
options.c: Avoid methods so every object exposes all of its data directly.
options.d: Copy the same logic into each subclass to avoid shared code.

concept.title: Capstone Scope and Architecture: the idea
concept.bodyMarkdown: **Capstone Scope and Architecture** is not just syntax. In this course it is practiced inside small projects where one file owns one clear part of the program. Plan a real multi-file OOP app before writing the final classes and services.

multifile-shape.title: Capstone Scope and Architecture: the file shape
multifile-shape.bodyMarkdown: The file layout matters. Models hold object state and behavior, services coordinate groups of objects, tests verify behavior, and `main.py` stays thin enough to read.

graded-habit.title: Capstone Scope and Architecture: what the grader checks
graded-habit.bodyMarkdown: The grader checks behavior from the running project. A strong answer keeps imports working, keeps all files present, and proves the object behavior through output or assertions.
